does: This paper introduces safety token regularization (STR), a lightweight fine-tuning method that preserves safety alignment in large language models by identifying and constraining logits of salient safety tokens from rejection templates. The approach operates directly in logit space during training, preventing degradation of safety behaviors when models are fine-tuned on new domains or datasets. The method is computationally efficient, integrating seamlessly with parameter-efficient approaches like LoRA, and requires minimal additional implementation overhead compared to full fine-tuning or more complex alignment techniques. Experimental results demonstrate that STR maintains safety performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods while preserving task-specific utility and improving training stability.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in existing safety alignment literature, which has predominantly focused on pretraining procedures rather than the fine-tuning phase. Prior research established that well-aligned models can lose safety properties through domain-specific fine-tuning, yet few solutions directly targeted this post-training phase. The paper builds on foundational work in RLHF and preference optimization methods for alignment, but diverges by proposing a simpler, more direct approach using logit constraints rather than reward modeling or preference learning. The method also connects to emerging work on parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques and the mechanistic understanding of how safety information is encoded in model representations, extending insights about behavioral preservation during adaptation.
enables: This approach opens several research directions for robust model adaptation in safety-critical applications. By demonstrating that safety can be preserved through lightweight logit-space constraints, the work enables practical deployment scenarios where models must be continually adapted to new domains while maintaining alignment guarantees. Future work could explore automated discovery of safety-critical tokens across diverse model architectures, theoretical characterization of when logit constraints sufficiently preserve safety semantics, and scaling the method to multimodal or extremely large models. The minimal computational overhead suggests potential for real-time safety intervention and integration into production fine-tuning pipelines, while the logit-space focus could inform more interpretable safety mechanisms and enable more sophisticated analysis of how safety knowledge degrades during adaptation.
